title: Chapter 30: The Frequency
content: The apartment smelled like old wood and someone else's life.

Maya sat cross-legged on the floor of the Bedford-Stuyvesant walkup, her back against a radiator that hadn't worked since the Obama administration, and tried to remember what it felt like to not be afraid. The floorboards were original — hundred-year-old pine, according to the friend-of-a-friend who'd taken the cash and asked no questions. They creaked when she shifted, which meant she'd learned to sit still. Stillness was the currency of hiding.

No phone. No laptop. No digital signature of any kind. The FBI warrant was live — she'd seen the headlines on Chloe's secure channel before ditching her own devices at Penn Station. *Maya Reyes, wanted for questioning in connection with the Aeterna Technologies security breach.* Her face, or a version of it, staring back from CNN and Fox and the New York Times app. A surveillance still from a parking garage in Palo Alto, chin blurred, eyes sharp. They'd chosen an unflattering frame. She looked like a criminal. Maybe she was one.

The only electronics in the apartment were the ones Chloe had couriered: a hardened tablet with a secure uplink, a signal-scrambled earpiece, and a portable shortwave radio. The radio was Chloe's idea. *"If everything else dies — internet, cellular, satellite — shortwave still works. It's the oldest broadcast technology we have. And it's untraceable if you know how to use it."*

Maya hadn't known how to use it. She'd learned. Three days of sitting in this dim room with the curtains drawn, turning the dial in micro-gradations, learning the topography of the shortwave band. The numbers stations. The automated weather broadcasts. The eerie, unplaceable tones that might have been military or might have been ghosts. She'd filled a notebook with frequencies and timestamps, a cartography of the invisible.

The apartment had one window, facing an airshaft. Through it, she could see a sliver of Brooklyn sky — gunmetal gray, November, the kind of light that made everything look like it had already happened. Dust motes swam in the meager glow. The radiator ticked, expansion and contraction, like a mechanical heartbeat. She'd left the lights off. Darkness was safer. Darkness was protocol.

She checked the tablet. Jiordi's marker was active — Manhattan, somewhere in Midtown, prepping the global press network. He'd been methodical about it, building the contact list journalist by journalist, ensuring that when the moment came, the story wouldn't die in a single outlet's inbox. Five continents. Forty-seven newsrooms. A dead man's switch that would fire whether any of them were still alive to pull it or not.

Chloe's marker was moving — somewhere over Nebraska, if the trajectory held. She'd boarded a red-eye out of SFO with a forged passport and a backpack full of hardware designed to hijack a NYSE production truck. The absurdity of it didn't escape Maya. One woman, one backpack, one plan to reach 4.2 million phones simultaneously. The math was insane. The math was also their only option.

And Lucy. Lucy's marker was dark. It had been dark for days. The last confirmed ping was from Site C, three weeks ago, before Vlad's people sealed the internal network. Since then: nothing. Lucy Chen was a ghost inside a machine that Maya couldn't penetrate.

She picked up the shortwave radio. Old habit now, fingers finding the dial without looking, tuning to the frequency she'd been monitoring since she arrived. 14.247 MHz. Nothing but static. She listened anyway. Static was information. Static meant the band was clear. Static meant no one was transmitting, or no one was transmitting *yet*.
Four AM. Cell Block 3, Site C.

The guards changed shift at 0400 exactly. Four minutes of corridor overlap — the outgoing team at the checkpoint, the incoming team still suiting up in the anteroom. Four minutes where the surveillance looped on its recorded feed and no live eyes watched the monitors. Lucy had timed it fourteen times before she trusted the pattern. Fourteen nights of lying still, counting seconds, mapping the margins of the invisible.
Tonight was night fifteen.

She swung her legs off the cot. The concrete was cold through thin socks — they'd taken her shoes weeks ago, standard protocol for "long-term residents." She moved to the wall where the intercom panel was mounted. Standard institutional hardware, the kind you'd find in any hospital or correctional facility. A speaker, a microphone, a call button that no longer connected to anything human. But the wiring behind it — that was different. The intercom was patched into Site C's internal antenna array, a relic from the building's previous life as a DARPA research station. Someone had repurposed it for internal communications. No one had thought to disable the shortwave relay.
Lucy pressed the call button and held it. The relay clicked. The frequency opened.
She leaned close to the microphone, her lips almost touching the plastic mesh, and spoke.

"This is Lucy Chen. Day thirty-seven. I'm in Site C, Sublevel B, Cell Block 3. I don't know the geographic coordinates. Underground. The air recirculates every twelve minutes — I've counted. The water tastes like copper." She paused, steadied her breath. The microphone picked up the sound and turned it into a whisper of static that rode the carrier wave out into the world.

"They took blood from me every night for thirty-one days. They called it 'processing.' I call it what it is — a farm. Eight of us in this block. Two have been moved. I don't know where. The others — I can hear them through the walls sometimes. Coughing. Crying. One of them sings in what I think is Korean. I've never seen her face."

She closed her eyes. The words were getting easier and harder at the same time. Easier because she'd practiced them, shaped them in her head during the long hours of fluorescent nothing. Harder because each night she had to decide how much truth the ether could carry. How much of herself she could bear to broadcast into the void.

"I'm not livestock. My name is Lucy Chen. I'm AB negative. That's why they want me. That's why I'm still alive." Her voice cracked, and she let it. Cracks were evidence too. "But I'm not just alive. I'm fighting. If anyone can hear this—"

The relay clicked off. The four minutes were over. She heard the anteroom door open, the shuffle of boots, the low murmur of the incoming shift. She was back on the cot before the first guard passed her cell, eyes closed, breathing measured, a woman who had learned to disappear on command.

But her voice was still moving. Riding the shortwave band at 299,792 kilometers per second, bouncing off the ionosphere, refracting through the atmosphere's layers, attenuating with distance but not dying. Never quite dying. Sound into signal into light into wave, her testimony arcing over mountains and plains and rivers, a voice in the dark that might reach no one or might reach everyone.
The radio crackled.

Maya's hand stopped on the dial. She'd been cycling through the band aimlessly, half-listening, half-dozing, when the static shifted — a change in texture, a tightening, as if the frequency itself had inhaled. She knew that sound. She'd been waiting for it without admitting she was waiting.
She pressed the radio closer. The signal was faint, distorted by atmospheric interference and the cheap speaker, but the words were there. A voice. *Her* voice.

"...took blood from me every night for thirty-one days. They called it 'processing.' I call it what it is — a farm. I'm not livestock. My name is Lucy Chen. I'm AB negative. That's why they want me. That's why I'm still alive. But I'm not just alive. I'm fighting. If anyone can hear this—"

The signal collapsed back into static. Gone. The ionosphere had swallowed the rest, or the relay had cut out, or the distance had simply won. But the words were already inside Maya, lodged like shrapnel.

Her hands were shaking. The radio trembled against her palms, and she gripped it harder, pressing it against her sternum as if she could push Lucy's voice through her ribs and keep it there forever. The plastic was warm from her skin and the faint heat of the internal electronics. She could feel the speaker still vibrating against her chest, tiny oscillations, the ghost of a waveform.

Lucy was alive. Lucy was *speaking*. Not a marker on a screen, not a data point, not a frozen image from a security feed — a voice, warm and cracked and furious, broadcasting her own existence into the world. The courage of it collapsed Maya. She sat on the hundred-year-old pine floorboards in a stranger's apartment in Brooklyn, and she cried. Not the controlled, functional tears of someone managing stress. These were the deep, sudden, salt-tasting tears that come from a place below thought. They ran down her cheeks and pooled at the corners of her mouth — warm, then cooling, then gone into the dust of the apartment. She tasted them without wiping them away. They tasted like relief and terror and love, all the things she'd been holding in a fist behind her sternum since the night Lucy vanished.

She hadn't let herself imagine this moment. Hoping was a vulnerability. Hoping meant the possibility of confirming the worst. But now Lucy was out there — not out there, *in here*, in the radio, in the air, in the frequency — and the hope had breached every wall Maya had built.
She wiped her face with the back of her hand. Sat up. Placed the radio carefully on the floor in front of her, the dial still set to 14.247 MHz, the speaker hissing its gentle static.
Then she reached for the tablet.

Kapoor's message arrived on the secure channel seventeen minutes later, timestamped 0347 PST. She was still inside Aeterna. Still functional. The readout was clinical, the way Kapoor always was when she was scared.

*Firewall upgrade deployed 0300. Broadcast hijack window now <70 seconds. Repeat: 70 seconds maximum for Lumina push. Window opens at market open bell, closes when the intrusion detection auto-resolves.*

*Additional intel: Vlad has ordered all Sublevel B and Site C specimens sedated on IPO morning. Procedure begins 0500 local. Standard pharmacological cocktail — midazolam and fentanyl. Duration: 6-8 hours. He doesn't want any disruption during the bell ceremony.*

Maya read the message twice. The sedation order was a clock inside a clock. If Lucy was sedated at 0500 on IPO morning, she couldn't transmit. Her voice — that extraordinary, impossible voice — would be silenced before it could reach the world at the exact moment the world was watching.
Seventy seconds to push evidence to 4.2 million phones. Four minutes of unmonitored time for Lucy to broadcast her testimony. Two different windows, both closing, both measured in heartbeats.
She typed a response to Kapoor: *Confirmation received. Maintaining position. Do not engage the sedation order — your cover is priority.*
Then she turned to the transmitter.

The network was still active — Chloe's distributed relay, the one Vlad thought he'd disabled when he seized Chloe's original nodes. He'd cut the head off a snake that grew new heads. The relay was decentralized now, bouncing signals through a mesh of compromised IoT devices, smart home hubs, and one very confused smart refrigerator in a co-working space in Austin. It shouldn't work. It worked anyway.
Maya set the transmitter to Lucy's frequency. 14.247 MHz. She pressed the broadcast button and spoke clearly, slowly, each word enunciated against the static.

"Lucy. It's Maya. IPO day. Before they sedate you — transmit. Transmit everything. Your voice, your name, what they did. We'll carry you to the world. But you have to transmit BEFORE they give you the sedative. 4 AM. IPO day. That's your window. 4 AM. Transmit everything. I'll be listening. We'll all be listening."

She released the button. The transmission burst out into the ether, a signal heading west, bouncing, refracting, diminishing — but moving. Whether Lucy would receive it depended on the relay, the ionosphere, the antenna at Site C, and four minutes of unmonitored time on a morning that hadn't happened yet. It depended on forces Maya couldn't control and variables she couldn't calculate.
She'd sent the message. Now all she could do was trust the frequency.
Maya lay down on the floor.

The wood was hard beneath her shoulder blades, unyielding, real in a way that nothing else was right now. The dust tickled her nose. Somewhere in the building, a pipe groaned. The radiator ticked its mechanical heartbeat. She placed the radio on her chest, the speaker centered over her sternum, and closed her eyes.

The static filled her ears. It was formless, infinite, the sound of every frequency carrying nothing and everything. Somewhere in that static was Lucy's voice, or the echo of it, or the memory of it. Somewhere in that static was a woman in an underground cell, speaking into a microphone that might reach no one, refusing to be silent. Somewhere in that static was the most important seventy seconds of Maya's life, waiting to happen.

Seven days until the IPO. Seven days until Vlad stood on a stage and rang a bell and pretended that Aeterna was everything the prospectus claimed. Seven days until 4.2 million phones lit up with evidence. Seven days until Lucy's voice, if she could transmit, if she could stay awake, if the guards' schedule held and the relay worked and the ionosphere cooperated — until Lucy's voice reached the world.
Seven days. A week. The space between a breath and a heartbeat.

Maya pressed the radio harder against her chest. She could feel the vibration of the static in her ribs, a low continuous tremor, like music played too quietly to hear but just loudly enough to feel. The plastic was warm. The weight was grounding.

In the dark of the Brooklyn apartment, with the curtains drawn and the lights off and her face still damp with tears, she whispered to the frequency. Not loud enough for the microphone to catch. Not loud enough for anyone but the void.
"I hear you, Luce. I hear you. And on November 15th, the whole world will too."
The static answered. It always answered.
She lay there, holding the radio, holding the frequency, holding the voice of her best friend as it crossed the continent in waves she couldn't see, and she did not let go.
